content.body: The circulatory system transports blood, nutrients, oxygen, hormones, and waste products throughout the body. Humans have a double circulatory system: blood passes through the heart twice for every complete circuit of the body.

The Heart:
• 4 chambers: Left Atrium, Right Atrium, Left Ventricle, Right Ventricle
• Valves prevent backflow of blood
• Left side pumps oxygenated blood to body (systemic circulation)
• Right side pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs (pulmonary circulation)
• Heart rate (resting): ~70 beats per minute

content.title: Blood Vessels
content.body:
Arteries:
• Carry blood AWAY from the heart
• Thick muscular walls to withstand high pressure
• Carry oxygenated blood (except pulmonary artery)

Veins:
• Carry blood TOWARD the heart
• Thin walls, valves to prevent backflow
• Carry deoxygenated blood (except pulmonary vein)

Capillaries:
• Tiny vessels one cell thick
• Site of exchange — O₂ and nutrients diffuse out; CO₂ and waste diffuse in

content.title: Blood Components
content.body:
Red blood cells (erythrocytes): carry oxygen using haemoglobin; no nucleus; biconcave shape for large surface area
White blood cells (leucocytes): fight infection (some engulf bacteria; some produce antibodies)
Platelets: small fragments that help blood clot at wounds
Plasma: liquid part (~55% of blood); carries dissolved substances — glucose, CO₂, urea, hormones, proteins

Blood pressure: the force of blood against artery walls. Normal: ~120/80 mmHg.
Hypertension (high blood pressure) increases risk of heart attack and stroke.
Factors increasing blood pressure: obesity, smoking, high salt diet, stress, lack of exercise.

questions.question: How many chambers does the human heart have?
questions.explanation: The human heart has 4 chambers: right atrium, left atrium, right ventricle, left ventricle.

questions.question: Which blood vessels carry blood AWAY from the heart?
questions.explanation: Arteries carry blood away from the heart. Veins carry blood toward the heart.

questions.question: What is the function of haemoglobin?
options: Carries oxygen in red blood cells
questions.answer: Carries oxygen in red blood cells
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that binds to oxygen and transports it around the body.

questions.question: Where does exchange of O₂ and CO₂ between blood and tissues occur?
questions.explanation: Capillaries have walls only one cell thick — perfect for diffusion of O₂ and nutrients to cells, and CO₂ and waste back to blood.

questions.question: Veins have valves to:
questions.explanation: Veins carry blood at low pressure back to the heart. Valves prevent blood from flowing backwards.

questions.question: Which component of blood fights infection?
questions.explanation: White blood cells (leucocytes) are part of the immune system — they engulf bacteria and produce antibodies.

questions.question: Platelets function to:
questions.explanation: Platelets are small cell fragments that clump together at wound sites to form clots and stop bleeding.

questions.question: The pulmonary artery carries blood:
questions.explanation: Pulmonary artery: heart → lungs (deoxygenated). Pulmonary vein: lungs → heart (oxygenated).

questions.question: Why do red blood cells have a biconcave (dimpled) shape?
options: To increase surface area for oxygen diffusion
To carry more haemoglobin per cell
questions.answer: To increase surface area for oxygen diffusion
questions.explanation: The biconcave disc shape maximises surface area for gas exchange and allows the cell to flex through narrow capillaries.

questions.question: High blood pressure (hypertension) is a risk factor for:
questions.explanation: High blood pressure damages artery walls and makes the heart work harder, increasing risk of heart attack and stroke.
